skill.name: pitch-deck-visuals
skill.description: Guides users through investor pitch deck structure, slide design rules, chart choices, and example visual generation workflows for fundraising presentations.
skill.summary: Create investor pitch deck visuals with slide frameworks, design rules, and chart guidance.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many shell, network, sensitive-file, and cryptography alerts in SKILL.md. Review confirms real risk from third-party CLI installation, network use, and remote command examples, while the Windows SAM and weak-crypto alerts are false positives from pitch-deck terminology and a SHA-256 checksum note. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party CLI Installation and Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: SKILL.md instructs users to install and run the inference.sh CLI, including a pipe-to-shell installer and multiple infsh app run commands. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it creates supply-chain, network, and external-command risk if users run the examples without verification.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly contains installation and execution commands. The commands align with the skill purpose, so this is a real operational risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Remote Python Execution Examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: Several examples send embedded Python code to infsh/python-executor for chart rendering. The examples appear benign and generate charts, but remote code execution services can expose user data or execute unreviewed code if prompts are modified.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly pass Python source code to a remote executor. The included code only renders charts, but the execution model is inherently elevated risk.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File Alerts Are Pitch-Deck Terminology
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The Windows SAM detections correspond to TAM/SAM/SOM market-sizing text, not references to the Windows SAM database or credential files. No evidence found of credential access or local sensitive-file reads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding lines are clearly about market sizing abbreviations. There is no filesystem path, credential database access, or command that targets Windows SAM.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The weak-cryptography detections are not cryptographic implementations. The only checksum context states SHA-256 verification, and the other flagged lines are pitch-deck guidance such as slide counts, design rules, and investor presentation advice.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found presentation copy and a SHA-256 checksum note, not MD5, SHA-1, DES, or custom cryptographic code. The static labels do not match the semantic context.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alert Not Supported by Context
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The flagged line is a design guideline that says to avoid excessive colors. No evidence found of OS, process, network, or environment reconnaissance commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is plain presentation-design advice. It contains no command, API, or instruction that gathers host information.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe-to-Shell Installer
dangerous_patterns.description: The quick-start command pipes a remote install script into sh. Even with a later checksum note, this pattern can execute unreviewed remote code on the user machine.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell pattern is present on the referenced line. This is a well-known supply-chain risk even when the provider is legitimate.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Service Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly invokes infsh app run for HTML rendering, Python chart generation, and image generation. These examples require network access and trust in a third-party execution service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is repeated and central to the skill examples. The supplied payloads are benign, but the execution path is external and should be disclosed.

content.value_statement: Founders often struggle to turn startup strategy into concise investor slides. This skill provides deck structure, visual rules, and chart patterns for clearer fundraising presentations.
actual_capabilities: Defines a 12-slide investor pitch deck framework with timing guidance.
Gives typography, layout, color, and slide-density rules for 1920x1080 decks.
Recommends chart types for market size, traction, competition, and key metrics.
Provides example CLI prompts for generating slide backgrounds, charts, and team avatars.
Lists common investor presentation mistakes and practical fixes.
limitations: Does not validate business claims, market data, or investor readiness.
Depends on inference.sh CLI examples for automated visual generation.
Does not create a complete editable PowerPoint or Keynote file by itself.
Example company names, metrics, and sources must be replaced with real data.

use_cases.title: Seed Fundraising Deck
use_cases.description: Structure a concise seed-stage deck with problem, solution, market, traction, team, and ask slides.

use_cases.title: Demo Day Presentation
use_cases.description: Convert a longer business story into a focused six-minute pitch with strong visual hierarchy.

use_cases.title: Grant or Partnership Pitch
use_cases.description: Use investor-style slide discipline to explain impact, traction, market need, and funding use.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Basic Deck Outline
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 12-slide investor pitch deck outline for my startup. Include the purpose of each slide and the key message to show.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Slide Visual Hierarchy
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my slide list and rewrite each slide as one main visual idea with a short headline, one supporting point, and design guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Design Metrics and Charts
prompt_templates.prompt: Recommend the best chart type for these fundraising metrics. Explain which metrics should be shown, hidden, or converted into a single key number.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare an Investor-Ready Visual System
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a complete visual direction for my investor deck, including typography, colors, chart styles, image guidance, and slide-by-slide layout rules.

output_examples.input: A founder needs a seed-stage fundraising deck for a reporting automation startup.
output: A 12-slide deck structure with timing for each section.
Problem and traction slides centered on one strong metric each.
Market, competition, team, financials, ask, and contact slide guidance.

output_examples.input: A team wants to replace dense slides with investor-friendly visuals.
output: Shorter slide headlines and fewer bullets.
A line chart for traction and concentric circles for TAM/SAM/SOM.
Consistent margins, font sizes, colors, and source captions.

output_examples.input: A demo day participant needs a six-minute presentation format.
output_examples.output: A focused visual plan that limits slide count, highlights traction, reduces product-demo time, and ends with a clear ask.

best_practices: Replace all sample metrics, sources, and company names before using the guidance.
Verify any CLI install script manually before running commands from the examples.
Keep each slide focused on one investor question and one primary visual.
anti_patterns: Do not paste confidential fundraising data into third-party services without approval.
Do not use feature matrices when a positioning map would tell a clearer story.
Do not expand the framework into a long deck with repeated product details.

faq.question: Does this skill create a full pitch deck file?
faq.answer: No. It provides structure, design rules, and visual generation examples. You still assemble the final deck in your presentation tool.

faq.question: Can I use it for a Series A deck?
faq.answer: Yes. The framework supports seed and Series A decks, but later-stage decks need stronger traction and financial detail.

faq.question: Does it check whether my market data is correct?
faq.answer: No. You must verify market size, customer metrics, revenue, and sources before presenting to investors.

faq.question: Why is this skill marked medium risk?
faq.answer: It includes third-party CLI, network, and remote execution examples. Review commands before running them.

faq.question: Can it help with non-startup presentations?
faq.answer: Yes. The visual rules can help grant, partnership, and internal strategy presentations that need a clear funding story.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
